coredata.dc:title: A novel microfluidic paper-based colorimetric sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer membranes for highly selective and sensitive detection of bisphenol A
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Qingkun
Wang, Yanhu
Zhang, Lina
Ge, Shenguang
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: A novel microfluidic paper-based colorimetric sensor for the detection of bisphenol A (BPA) was developed based on the intrinsic peroxidase activity of ZnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles (ZnFe2O4 MNPs) and the adsorption capacity of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) membranes. In the process, ZnFe2O4 MNPs and cellulose fibers in paper were wrapped by MIP membranes (ZnFe2O4@MIP membranes) successfully. Novel MIP membranes for the recognition of BPA were prepared in polar solvent using acrylamide as the functional monomer and BPA as the template by bulk polymerization, which resulted in a new type of BPA imprinted polymer membranes. Adsorption isotherms model of MIP membranes was employed to describe the isotherms, and maximum adsorption capacity was evaluated. In the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), the as-prepared ZnFe2O4 MNPs peroxidase mimetics was used to catalyze chromogenic reactions and showed the color with respective intensity. Under optimized conditions, the grey intensity was found to be proportional to the BPA concentrations in the range of 10 nM–1000nM (R2 =0.9945) with a detection limit of 6.18nM. With the advantages of highly reproducible response, good selectivity and excellent regeneration, the colorimetric sensor holds great potential for portable detection of targets in environmental monitoring, security inspection and complicated matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer membranes
ZnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles
Microfluidic paper-based colorimetric sensor
Catalyze chromogenic reactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the colorimetric sensor on μPADs
BPA detection with the colorimetric sensor
Characterization of MIP membranes, MIP membranes@ZnFe2O4 and NIP membranes
Selectivity, reproducibility and regeneration of sensor
Mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of ZnFe2O4 MNPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Special Fund for Shandong Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Special Fund for Shandong Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation (No. 2014ZZCX02703 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21475052 , 21277058 ). Qingkun Kong studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student. Yanhu Wang studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as PhD student. Lina Zhang received her PhD degree in Civil Engineering from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology in 2014. She then joined University of Jinan, where currently she is an Assistant Professor in School of Material Science and Engineering. Her current scientific interests focused on nanomaterials, analytical chemistry and material engineering. Shenguang Ge received his PhD degree in Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in 2013 from Shandong University, completed his master degree studies in University of Jinan in 2006. He joined University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His research interests are in the area of biosensor and chemsensor. Jinghua Yu received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2003 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, China. She is currently positioned as a professor at University of Jinan. She spends most of her time investigating biomedical engineering, especially for the development of biosensor devices and analytical tools.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication procedure for the paper-based colorimetric sensor.
Possible mechanism for the ZnFe2O4-MIP membranes-TMB system.
(A) TEM and (B) XRD spectrum of the as-synthesized ZnFe2O4 MNPs.
SEM image of (A) pure cellulose paper, inset is the SEM image of pure paperfibers, (B) the as-synthesized MIP membranes, (C) MIP membranes@ZnFe2O4 and (D) NIP membranes.

(A) FT-IR analysis of (a) ZnFe2O4 MNPs, (b) μPADs, (c) MIP membranes@μPADs and (d) ZnFe2O4@MIP membranes@μPADs. (B) XRD spectrum of (a) ZnFe2O4 modified μPADs@MIP membranes (b) μPADs, (c) μPADs@ ZnFe2O4@MIP membranes.
(A) Equilibrium data and modeling for the adsorption of BPA onto MIP membranes and NIP membranes. (B) Scatchard plot of the adsorption isotherm of BPA to the MIP membranes-BPA at equilibrium.
(A): Effect of the pH, (B): Effect of the temperature, and (C): Effect of the H2O2 concentration.
Effects of (a) adsorption time, (b) washing time on grey intensity.
The calibration curves for the determination of target BPA through paper-based colorimetric sensor.
(A) Selectivity of sensor for BPA and its analogues. (B) Regeneration of the colorimetric sensor.
The results of comparing with other reported techniques for the determination of BPA.

head.ce:title: A novel microfluidic paper-based colorimetric sensor based on molecularly imprinted polymer membranes for highly selective and sensitive detection of bisphenol A

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel microfluidic paper-based colorimetric sensor for the detection of bisphenol A (BPA) was developed based on the intrinsic peroxidase activity of ZnFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles (ZnFe2O4 MNPs) and the adsorption capacity of molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) membranes. In the process, ZnFe2O4 MNPs and cellulose fibers in paper were wrapped by MIP membranes (ZnFe2O4@MIP membranes) successfully. Novel MIP membranes for the recognition of BPA were prepared in polar solvent using acrylamide as the functional monomer and BPA as the template by bulk polymerization, which resulted in a new type of BPA imprinted polymer membranes. Adsorption isotherms model of MIP membranes was employed to describe the isotherms, and maximum adsorption capacity was evaluated. In the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), the as-prepared ZnFe2O4 MNPs peroxidase mimetics was used to catalyze chromogenic reactions and showed the color with respective intensity. Under optimized conditions, the grey intensity was found to be proportional to the BPA concentrations in the range of 10 nM–1000nM (R2 =0.9945) with a detection limit of 6.18nM. With the advantages of highly reproducible response, good selectivity and excellent regeneration, the colorimetric sensor holds great potential for portable detection of targets in environmental monitoring, security inspection and complicated matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Gore reported endocrine disruptors including pesticides and other chemicals presented in children and personal care products, electronics, clothing and food packaging materials can be concerned with our daily life contact materials [1]. Bisphenol A (BPA) as an environmental endocrine disrupting chemical is used widely in plastic food packaging products, such as baby bottles and plastic beverage bottles, resulting in the contamination of food and the environment [2]. Studies demonstrate that BPA rapidly crosses the placenta and distributes in both placental and fetal tissues, such that fetal levels in certain tissues surpass maternal serum levels shortly after dosage [3]. Therefore, the determination of such BPA residues has become an important task. Various approaches have been proposed for the determination of BPA residues in environment, mainly based on electrochemical measurements [4], liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry [5], fluorescence [6] and liquid chromatogram [7]. Athough methods are sensitive, they usually require extensive sample purification and pretreatment, expensive instrumentation, and high level technical expertise. There is a great desire to develop a portable strategy for detection of BPA.

Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) have been introduced into assay as promising tool for detection of analyte due to their low-cost, ease-to-operate, portable platforms and disposability [8–10]. The porous structure of cellulose paper provides a large surface-to-volume ratio, which allows reagent storage by simply drying the wetted area [11]. Paper-based microdevices also provide many rapid and robust advantages for detection. Furthermore, the fabrication of paper-based micro-devices is simple, efficient via a variety of well developed processes, including inkjet printing [12], wax printing [13], or screen printing [14]. These paper based analytical devices are usually integrated with simple colorimetric detection systems [15]. Detection of target analyte by means of colorimetric sensors is based on the change in color due to signal–analyte interaction. To improve the accuracy of the detection without of large equipment, colorimetric method based on analyzing color value of photographs, has been reported by many researchers [16–18]. Recently, a series of nanomaterials exhibit intrinsic peroxidase-like activity with good catalytic properties and wide range of practical applications are becoming a significant field, such as CeO2 NPs [19], CuO NPs [20], CoFe2O4 NPs [21], and V2O5 nanorods [22]. Besides metal oxide nanomaterials, zinc ferrite magnetite nanoparticles (ZnFe2O4 MNPs) have long attracted interest because of its a promising peroxidase mimetic with advantages of stability, dispersibility, ease of separation and low toxicity of zinc ions compared to other metallic cations [23–26]. Meanwhile, as a mimic peroxidase, ZnFe2O4 MNPs exhibit good catalytic properties compared to other peroxidase nanomimetics and horseradish peroxidase [27]. Therefore, there is an increasing interest in the development of simple and low-cost paper-based colorimetric sensors for the highly sensitive and selective detection of BPA that can allow reliable on-site real-time detection.

The central part of a chemical or biosensor is the recognition element, which is in close contact with an interrogative transducer [28]. The molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) technique is an effective and robust method for obtaining a recognition element for incorporation in chemical and biological sensors [29]. This is because that molecularly imprinted polymers are in fact materials which possess highly selective recognition properties acquired during their synthesis consisting in polymerization in the presence of the target analyte. An imprint containing reactive groups with binding capability remains behind in the polymer. It is now interesting to develop novel and efficient analysis platforms using the features of molecularly imprinted polymers.

In the present study, we tried to combine the adsorption capacity of MIP membranes with ZnFe2O4 as peroxidase mimetics as well as its colorimetric potential to fabricate functional paper for detection of BPA. Successful modification of ZnFe2O4 and cellulose fibers in paper wrapped by MIP membranes would lead to cavities for the binding of BPA with higher performance, and subsequent color development by H2O2 and TMB might offer better detecting assay for colorimetric measurement of BPA. The resulting complex (TMB) could then be oxidized by OH which was generated through ZnFe2O4 MNPs reacted with H2O2, leading to an obvious color change. MIP membranes with high surface area not only provided much more multidimensional spaces suitable for the absorption of BPA, but also had an ability to specifically bind the target analyte. These results demonstrated an excellent colorimetric sensor with high selectivity, sensitivity and stability. And our design could achieve the visual quantitative detection without any expensive instruments.

All chemicals were commercially available as analytical reagent. Hydrazine hydrate, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), ethanol, methanol and acetic acid were purchased from The Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Whatman chromatography paper #1 (200.0mm×200.0mm) (pure cellulose paper) was obtained from GE Healthcare Worldwide (Pudong Shanghai, China) and used with further adjustment of size. H2O2 (30%, w/v), polyethylene glycol-4000, NaAc, tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) and diethylstilbestrol (DES) were obtained from Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), zinc oxide (ZnO), FeCl3·6H2O, Hydroquinone (1,4-DHB), phenolphthalein (PP) and ascorbic acid were obtained from Sinopfarm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Bisphenol A (BPA), styrene, acrylamide (AA), Ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and free radical initiator benzoin ethyl ether (BEE) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore water purification system (resistivity ≥18.20 MV, MilliQ, Millipore) was used in all experiments.

The infrared spectra was obtained on a Thermo Nicolet 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. The phase characterization was performed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a D8 advance diffractometer system equipped with Cu Kα radiation (Bruker Co., Germany). The ZF5 Portable UV lamp was used as UV irradiation source. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was obtained using a JSM-6700F microscope (Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were performed using JEOL 4000 EX microscope.

The μPADs were fabricated using a method developed recently in our laboratory [30–33]. The screen with a specific pattern was placed close to a piece of chromatography paper. Adobe illustrator CS4 software was used to create the device design. This paper-based colorimetric sensor was comprised of two layers of patterned rectangular papers (named α-sheet and β-sheet respectively from up to down). The α-sheet was comprised of one working zone (6.0mm in diameter, 2mm edge-to-edge separation). One circular contacting zone (6.0mm in diameter) was designed on β-sheet, which was with the same position as the one working zone on α-sheet. Then a solid wax which was used as a squeegee was rubbed through the screen stencil to form wax patterns on the paper surface. The wax-screen-printed paper was placed in an oven set at 130°C for 150s, and then the wax melted and penetrated the thickness of the paper to create completely hydrophobic barriers in the paper to form the microzone. There was no need for any other complicated instruments.

Preparation of ZnFe2O4 MNPs was carried out according to the earlier described procedure [34]. A 4mL aqueous dispersion containing 0.024g of freshly prepared ZnO was homogeneously mixed with 0.3172g of FeCl3·6H2O and 0.053g of ascorbic acid by ultra-sonication for 30s. Then, 1mL of hydrazine hydrate was added into the above dispersion to form a homogeneous dark solution with magnetic stirring for about 20min. After that, the mixed solution was transferred and sealed into a 10mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, heated at 180°C in an electric oven for 12h, then cooled to room temperature naturally. The black products were obtained by centrifuging and washing the final precipitation with distilled water and ethanol several times, and then dried at 60°C for 6h for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the colorimetric sensor on μPADs
ce:section.ce:para: The paper-based colorimetric sensor was constructed by immobilizing the corresponding MIP membranes and ZnFe2O4 on one paper microzone through MIP membranes wrapping paper (Scheme 1 ). MIP membranes were prepared by bulk polymerization as reported in previous study [35–37]. The BPA MIP membranes were prepared by bulk polymerization using BPA, AA, EGDMA and BEE as template molecule, functional monomer, cross linker and initiator, respectively. BPA (0.1mmol) and AA (0.4mmol) were dissolved in 1.35mL of DMSO. The mixture was sonicated to facilitate the combination between template molecule and functional monomer by non-covalent interaction. Previously 0.2g of ZnFe2O4 and 1g of polyethylene glycol were dissolved in 3mL of ultrapure water. Then 0.15mL of EGDMA, 8mL of toluene, 8mL of styrene and BEE (0.03mmol) were added to above mixture and stirred with magnetic stirrer. A small flux of argon was maintained during the whole procedure to ensure an oxygen-free environment. The polymerization mixture was placed under a ZF5 Portable UV lamp 254nm and irradiated for 3h. Paper was washed successively by ethanol to remove the polymer physically adhered on the surface of MIP membranes and with the mixed methanol and acetic acid (v/v=9:1) to remove the template molecule. The reference composite NIP membranes were prepared and processed in the same way, but in the absence of template molecule. After that, solution with different concentration of target was added to the α-sheet and allowed to incubate for a few minutes at room temperature. Subsequently, TMB was applied to paper zone of the β-sheet at room temperature prior to use. Finally, the modified paper-based microzone was used to measure BPA via a colorimetric reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BPA detection with the colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The colorimetric detection of BPA could be achieved using functionalized MIP membranes@ZnFe2O4. 10μL sample solution containing different concentration of BPA was added to the corresponding zone on α-sheet and allowed to incubate for 30min. Then physically absorbed excess BPA was carefully rinsed with water. Next, 10μL of 20mM TMB was added to paper zone on β-sheet. After that α-sheet was placed over β-sheet, two papers were clamped to make them stacked closely. Subsequently, 5μL of 500mM H2O2 and 10μL of 0.2M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) were added to paper zone and incubated for 10min at 30°C and kept in an ice-water bath for 10min to terminate the reaction. Finally, a fast color change occurred, which could be easily judged by the naked eye on β-paper. The grey intensity was measured and used to construct the calibration curve which was used to determine the analytical performance of the sensor.

Fig. 1 A showed a representative TEM image of the as-prepared ZnFe2O4 MNPs. It could be seen that the nanoparticles were with a spherical shape and narrowly distributed with diameters in the range of 17–20nm. The crystallinity and structure of the ZnFe2O4 MNPs were confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1B, the diffraction peaks from inside to outside could be well indexed to (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), (620), (533) planes respectively, which corresponding to crystal planes of spinel ZnFe2O4 (JCPDS 22-1012). According to Sherrer’s formula, the average crystallite size, which was calculated based on the XRD pattern (311) was approximately 19nm, which is in agreement with the value determined by TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MIP membranes, MIP membranes@ZnFe2O4 and NIP membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, pure cellulose paper was used as substrate to fabricate a portable, simple the colorimetric device. The morphologies of the modified paper were characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the pure cellulose paper revealed smooth microfibers. Meanwhile, the pure cellulose paper was porous structures (the inset in Fig. 2A), indicating solution was permeated through paper-based analytical devices easily. The images in Fig. 2B revealed MIP membranes were synthesized successfully. It could be seen that cellulose fibers were wrapped with molecular imprinted membranes uniformly. It was rough by comparing the pure cellulose paper in Fig. 2A. MIP membranes had large surface area, indicating that large number of effective imprinting sites could exist in the surface to rebind the template molecules in aqueous media [38]. From Fig. 2C we could see that there were no obvious differences between the morphology of MIP membranes and ZnFe2O4@MIP membranes. Noticeably, the MIP membranes displayed a rough outside polymeric surface (Fig. 2C), and in contrast, the surface of NIP membranes was still smooth (Fig. 2D), which indicated that there were a large number of exposed complementary imprinted cavities on the surface of MIP membranes while no recognition sites on the surface of NIP membranes.

ZnFe2O4 MNPs and cellulose fibers in paper wrapped by MIP membranes were evidenced by FT-IR analysis and XRD. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the strong Fe-O absorption bands around 552cm−1 and ZnO bands at 427cm−1 (curve a) corroborated that the main phase of the as-prepared particles was zinc ferrite. The absorption in curve c and curve d at 891cm−1 could be assigned to twisting vibration of NH in MIP membranes. In Fig. 3B, it could be seen that the typical diffraction peaks of ZnFe2O4 (311) were observable (curve a). There were not typical peaks of ZnFe2O4 which were observable (curve b and c), indicating that there were no ZnFe2O4 MNPs exist in surface of MIP membranes. These results indicated that ZnFe2O4 MNPs were wrapped by MIP membranes successfully.

The binding characteristics of the MIP and the NIP were respectively investigated by equilibrium binding experiments. The absorption isotherm experiments for MIP membranes and NIP membranes were carried out that a series of MIP membranes and NIP membranes were immersed in the BPA concentration range of 10−7 to 10−9 M (Fig. 4 A). As it can be seen from Fig. 4A, not only the MIP membranes but also the NIP membranes could adsorb BPA in solution. However, the adsorption capacity of the MIP membranes was obviously larger than that of the NIP membranes. The obtained data was used for the Scatchard analysis to estimate the binding parameters of the MIP membranes. The Scatchard analysis is a graphical representation of the linearized form of the Langmuir equation. The Scatchard equation is given as Q/c=(Qmax−Q)/Kd, in which Kd is the equilibrium dissociation constant, Qmax is the apparent maximum number of binding sites, and c is the concentration of BPA in adsorbed solution [39,40]. The Scatchard plot was not linear within the whole BPA concentration range indicating that the binding sites in the MIP were non-uniform in Fig. 4B. However, it was observed that two distinct sections within the plot can be regarded as straight lines. This revealed that there were two classes of binding sites in the MIP membranes. The dissociation constants (Kd) and the apparent maximum number of binding sites (Qmax) determined by Scatchard analyses were 1.965×10−5 mol/L and 1.26×10−7 mol/g for high affinity sites of MIP membranes, while the Kd and Qmax values were 3.74×10−5 mol/L and 1.32×10−6 mol/g for the low affinity sites, respectively. The results demonstrated that at a lower concentration of BPA, the occupied binding sites possessed a higher affinity. On the other hand, at a higher concentration the analyte could bind to cavities of the polymers with higher density, but with a lower affinity.

The grey intensity of microfluidic paper-based colorimetric sensor was strongly dependent on pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration. The effect of pH on grey intensity was examined by buffer solution within the pH range of 3–6.5. Fig. 5 A showed the pH-dependent response curve which indicated that grey intensity was larger with increase of pH. The reason may be that few iron ions could be leached from the ZnFe2O4 of the sensor. When the pH value of the buffer solution was greater than 4, the catalytic oxidation of ZnFe2O4 decreased with the increment of pH. Therefore, 0.2M pH 4.0 acetate buffer solution was taken as the optimal buffer solution to obtain the high grey intensity. The effect of temperature on the catalytic reaction was investigated at different temperatures from 10 to 60°C as shown in Fig. 5B. The grey intensity of the sensor in the presence of TMB–H2O2 increased with increasing temperature. When the grey intensity reached to maximum at 30°C, result was changed at increasing of the temperature. In view of the stability and catalytic activity of TMB-H2O2-ZnFe2O4, 37°C was employed as the optimal temperature for further studies. When the H2O2 concentration was relatively low, the surface active sites of the sensor were not fully occupied by H2O2 and few OH radicals were generated in Fig. 5C. Abundant H2O2 was adsorbed on the sensor surface which could generate more OH radicals with concentration increasing. The optimal H2O2 concentration was achieved at 0.5M.

The adsorption time to influence on the grey intensity in the presence of 100nM of BPA was also demonstrated. It was necessary to optimize the reaction time of the sensor to further evaluate the sensitivity of the assay. As shown in Fig. 6 , the grey intensity of the sensor was decreased with the increasing time in the 0–25min time period (curve a). After more than 25min, the degree of grey intensity became stable with the reaction time prolonged. Consequently, considering the optimal analytical performance, the adsorption time of 30min was selected in the further study. In curve b, the impact of washing time on the grey intensity was investigated. The grey intensity reached a plateau at 50min because no further template BPA could be monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and regeneration of sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of the colorimetric sensor, the influence of some possible 100nM of interfering compounds (TBHQ, DES, 1,4-DHB, PP, mixture of interfering compounds) were examined. It could be seen from Fig. 8 A that BPA led to smaller grey intensity compared to the other compounds and the effect of four interferents was neglectable. This was because the recognition sites of imprinted cavities were complementary to BPA, resulting in less chance to react between ZnFe2O4 as catalyst and H2O2. The results implied that the MIP membrane had a high selectivity for BPA over other competitive analogue. Thus, it was possible for the sensor to monitor BPA in complicated matrices.

The reproducibility of the colorimetric sensor was evaluated with intra- and inter-assay precisions. The detection of 500nM of BPA with five independently fabricated papers (inter-assay) gave an RSD of 6.9%. A series of five sensors prepared from the same batch were used to evaluate the intra-assay precision at a concentration level of 500nM, the grey intensity was 125 with a calculated RSD of 8.4%. Thus, the results demonstrated acceptable precision and fabrication reproducibility.

Regeneration was of significance to colorimetric sensor. After templates were removed with the mixed methanol and acetic acid (v/v=9:1), the colorimetric sensor was used to detect 60nM of BPA repeatedly four times. As shown in Fig. 8B, the grey intensity of the colorimetric sensor was very similar after simples were washed with elution washing twice. The as-proposed colorimetric sensor exhibited excellent regeneration, which was of great application potential in point-of-care applications for accurate detection of BPA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of peroxidase-like activity of ZnFe2O4 MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: A possible colorimetric reaction mechanism of ZnFe2O4 MNPs-MIP membranes-H2O2 was presented in Scheme 2 according to other colorimetric sensing mechanism [27,45]. As for MIP membranes, there were recognition sites on the surface of MIP membranes. In the absence of BPA, H2O2 molecules could be adsorbed on the surface of ZnFe2O4 MNPs and activated by the bound Fe3+ to generate the OH, and the generated OH was stabilized by ZnFe2O4 MNPs via partial electron exchange interaction. TMB was oxidized by OH to form a blue color product, which came from the charge transfer complex consisting of a cation free radical and TMB. When BPA was anchored onto the surface of MIP membranes, H2O2 cannot react with ZnFe2O4 MNPs to generate the OH, indicating that sensor could realize grey detection of BPA based on MIP membranes.

A novel paper-based colorimetric sensor for the detection of BPA is developed based on the intrinsic peroxidase activity of ZnFe2O4 and cellulose paper wrapped with MIP membranes. Successful modification of ZnFe2O4 wrapped by MIP membranes in the cellulose paper would lead to cavities for the binding of BPA with higher performance, and subsequent color development by H2O2 and TMB might offer better detecting assay for colorimetric measurement of BPA. The color change can be observed by naked eyes and quantified by software. This sensing platform shows advantages of the low cost of paper based assay with the simplicity of colorimetric detection method and allows us to quantitative determination of BPA with a low detection limit 6.18nM with high selectivity, sensitivity and regeneration. The proposed method provides reliable strategy for detection of BPA with excellent sensitivity, which opens a very potential way for sensitive and selective detection at complicated matrix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Special Fund for Shandong Independent Innovation and Achievements Transformation (No. 2014ZZCX02703) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (21475052, 21277058).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fast method for bisphenol A and six analogues (S, F Z, P, AF, AP) determination in urine samples based on dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry

Preparation of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers for bisphenol A and its analogues and their application to the assay of bisphenol A in river water
Simple, distance-based measurement for paper analytical devices
Target-responsive DNA hydrogel mediated stop-flow microfluidic paper-based analytic device for rapid, portable and visual detection of multiple targets
Paper-based electrochemiluminescent 3D immunodevice for lab-on-paper specific, and sensitive point-of-care testing
Fabrication of novel transparent touch sensing device via drop-on-demand inkjet printing technique
Fabrication and characterization of paper-based microfluidics prepared in nitrocellulose membrane by wax printing
Printing of polymer microcapsules for enzyme immobilization on paper substrate
Paper based colorimetric biosensing platform utilizing cross-linked siloxane as probe

High-throughput sulfide sensing with colorimetric analysis of single Au-Ag core-shell nanoparticles

High-performance ZnCo2O4@CeO2 core@shell microspheres for catalytic CO oxidation

Cobalt ferrite-polyaniline heteroarchitecture: a magnetically recyclable photocatalyst with highly enhanced performances
Self-assembled vanadium pentoxide (V2O5) hollow microspheres from nanorods and their application in lithium-ion batteries

Ethanol monitoring by ZnFe2O4 thin film obtained by spray pyrolysis

Molecular imprinting: synthetic materials as substitutes for biological antibodies and receptors
A novel microfluidic origami photoelectrochemical sensor based on CdTe quantum dots modified molecularly imprinted polymer and its highly selective detection of S-fenvalerate
Dummy molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica prepared by hybrid imprinting method for solid-phase extraction of bisphenol A
Preparation and characterization of novel molecularly imprinted polymers based on thiourea receptors for nitrocompounds recognition
A novel and label-free immunosensor for bisphenol A using rutin as the redox probe
Electrochemical bisphenol A sensor based on exfoliated Ni2Al-layered double hydroxide nanosheets modified electrode Sens
An original ferroferric oxide and gold nanoparticles-modified glassy carbon electrode for the determination of bisphenol A
Electrochemical sensor based on magnetic molecularly imprinted nanoparticles at surfactant modified magnetic electrode for determination of bisphenol A
Paper-based colorimetric immunosensor for visual detection of carcinoembryonic antigen based on the high peroxidase-like catalytic performance of ZnFe2O4-multiwalled carbon nanotubes

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Qingkun Kong studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Yanhu Wang studies in school of chemistry and chemical engineering, University of Jinan as PhD student.

Lina Zhang received her PhD degree in Civil Engineering from The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology in 2014. She then joined University of Jinan, where currently she is an Assistant Professor in School of Material Science and Engineering. Her current scientific interests focused on nanomaterials, analytical chemistry and material engineering.

Shenguang Ge received his PhD degree in Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in 2013 from Shandong University, completed his master degree studies in University of Jinan in 2006. He joined University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His research interests are in the area of biosensor and chemsensor.

Jinghua Yu received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2003 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, China. She is currently positioned as a professor at University of Jinan. She spends most of her time investigating biomedical engineering, especially for the development of biosensor devices and analytical tools.
